summary:
A zero central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rate is unsustainable because global surveillance consistently records average incidence rates ranging from 0.8 to 4.1 per 1000 central line days, reflecting inherent risks driven by microbiological contamination and recurring human procedural errors. These underlying causes—including bacterial transfer through catheter placement and variability in staff performance—are fundamentally resistant to complete eradication within real-world medical settings. 

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{B}}$

The summary should be concise while capturing all essential facts related directly to the core issue raised in the original query about sustainability of zero CLABSI rates.

A zero central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rate is unsustainably high given documented global rates averaging between 0.8 and 4.1 per 1000 central line days. Persistent transmission occurs due to inevitable microorganisms colonizing catheters and recurrent human-related procedural mistakes, making absolute prevention impossible under routine care conditions.  

$\boxed{\text{B}}$

These intrinsic biological and operational limitations make total elimination clinically unrealistic. **$\boxed{\text{B}}$**
